literal: wisdom, discernment, insight; from pra- (intensive, 'forth, thoroughly') + √jñā 'to know' (pra-√jñā + a, fem.), 'a knowing-forth', heightened or thoroughgoing knowing

cognates.note: Only the √jñā root is cognate (PIE *ǵneh₃-); the prefix pra- (PIE *pro-, cf. Greek pró, Latin prō, Eng. ‘fore-/for-’) is a separate true cognate. The compound itself has no single Indo-European counterpart.

invariant_definition: *Prajñā* is wisdom in the strong sense: not the accumulation of information but the discerning insight that apprehends things as they are and orders one's response accordingly. The prefix *pra-* intensifies √jñā 'to know', so the term names a heightened or thoroughgoing knowing — discrimination, the settled understanding from which right action and right detachment are held to follow. It serves as a shared term across several traditions, with the object of insight differing by school. In the Upaniṣadic and Advaitic register *prajñā* names both the deep-sleep self — the *prājña* of the Māṇḍūkya, the 'mass of cognition' (*prajñāna-ghana*) that is the third quarter — and, more broadly, the wisdom that is identity-knowledge of *brahman*; the *mahāvākya* *prajñānaṃ brahma* ('consciousness is Brahman', Aitareya Upaniṣad 3.3) belongs to this family. In Pātañjala Yoga *prajñā* is the insight arising in deep absorption, culminating in *ṛtambharā prajñā*, the 'truth-bearing' wisdom said to grasp the particular as it is (YS 1.48–49). In Buddhist usage *prajñā* (Pali *paññā*) is the third of the three trainings — *śīla* (conduct), *samādhi* (concentration), *prajñā* (wisdom) — and the culminating *pāramitā* of the *prajñāpāramitā* literature, the insight into emptiness and no-self. The discerning person is also termed *vipaścit* ('the perceptive one'). *Prajñā* is thus the faculty whose stabilization the contemplative traditions take as their aim: wisdom that has become steady and unshaken.

school_framing.shared_core: *Prajñā* names **the discerning insight that sees the real as it is and steadies conduct upon that seeing** — wisdom as a faculty and an achievement, not mere information. The structural role of culminating cognition is widely shared; the object of that insight differs sharply by school.
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Real disagreement** over what *prajñā* finally discerns: the non-dual identity of self and *brahman* (Advaita), the isolation of *puruṣa* from *prakṛti* (Yoga), or the emptiness and selflessness of all dharmas (Buddhist). Same culminating-wisdom role, opposing accounts of its content. **Same concept, different aspect** between steadied practical wisdom (the Gītā's *sthita-prajña*) and the technical absorptive *prajñā* of Yoga.

per_school.definition: Advaita carries two related uses. In the Māṇḍūkya analysis *prājña* is the self in deep sleep, the mass of undifferentiated cognition (*prajñāna-ghana*), the third quarter — a state to be transcended toward the *turīya*. More broadly *prajñā* names the wisdom that is knowledge of *brahman*: the discerning insight, matured by hearing, reflection, and meditation (*śravaṇa-manana-nididhyāsana*), in which the identity of *ātman* and *brahman* is realized; *prajñānaṃ brahma* states consciousness itself as the absolute. Wisdom here is realization, not the acquisition of a new fact.

In Pātañjala Yoga *prajñā* is the insight that arises and deepens through the stages of absorption: as *nirvicāra* absorption clarifies, there dawns *adhyātma-prasāda* and a lucid *prajñā* (YS 1.47), culminating in *ṛtambharā prajñā*, the truth-bearing wisdom whose scope and grasp exceed inference and testimony because it apprehends the particular as it is (YS 1.48–49). This *prajñā* generates impressions that override other latent tendencies (YS 1.50); it is the cognitive fruit of *samādhi* and the proximate instrument of the discriminative discernment (*vivekakhyāti*) leading to *kaivalya*. The bhāṣya describes a sevenfold *prajñā* at the threshold of liberation (cf. YS 2.27).
per_school.register_tag: Φ+S; yogin (ṛtambharā prajñā, fruit of samādhi)

per_school.definition: *Prajñā* (Pali *paññā*) is the third of the three trainings — *śīla, samādhi, prajñā* — and the discernment held to liberate: insight into impermanence, suffering, and not-self, and, in the Mahāyāna *prajñāpāramitā* literature, into the emptiness (*śūnyatā*) of all dharmas. It is distinguished from concentration: *samādhi* steadies the mind, whereas *prajñā* is the cognition that sees, and on the classical analysis only this seeing uproots ignorance. As the last of the *pāramitās* it is treated as the perfection that orders the others toward liberation.
primary_loci: the three sikkhā (sīla-samādhi-paññā) of the suttas

per_school.definition: The *Gītā* forms from *prajñā* the compound *sthita-prajña* (2.54–55), one whose wisdom stands firm: practical-contemplative wisdom that becomes *pratiṣṭhitā* (well-established) when the senses are withdrawn and the mind is freed of craving, fear, and anger. The wise person is termed *vipaścit* (2.60), the discerning one — yet even his senses can carry the mind away if unguarded, so the accent of the teaching falls on the steadiness of *prajñā* rather than on the acquisition of further knowledge.

textual_history.summary: *Prajñā* grows from the Vedic verb pra-√jñā, 'to know forth, to discern the way'. In the Ṛgveda the form is verbal and participial — the Dawn 'knowing well the path of ṛta does not stray', the priest 'knowing' leads the offering — and the cognate cluster *dhī*, *manīṣā*, *kratu* carries the noetic weight; an abstract feminine *prajñā* is not yet a fixed term there. The decisive nominalisation is Upaniṣadic: the Aitareya gathers every cognitive name — perception, will, memory, design — under *prajñāna* and culminates in *prajñānaṃ brahma*, 'consciousness/discernment is Brahman' (Aitareya 3.3, one of the *mahāvākyas*), while the Māṇḍūkya fixes *prājña* as the technical name of the deep-sleep self, the 'mass of cognition' (*prajñānaghana*). Pātañjala Yoga then makes *prajñā* a graded attainment of absorption, climaxing in *ṛtambharā prajñā*, 'truth-bearing insight' (YS 1.48). The *Gītā* coins *sthita-prajña*, 'one whose insight stands firm' (2.54–55), shifting the accent from the content of wisdom to its steadiness. The line from Veda to Gītā is genuine for the *root* and its sense of discerning knowledge; the abstract noun and its specialised senses (deep-sleep knower, absorptive insight) are Upaniṣadic and post-Upaniṣadic developments, not Ṛgvedic data.

stages.locus: RV 1.124.3 (Aufrecht ed.; the verse recurs verbatim at RV 5.80.4)
stages.sanskrit: eṣā divo duhitā praty adarśi jyotir vasānā samanā purastāt | ṛtasya panthām anv eti sādhu prajānatīva na diśo mināti
stages.rendering: This daughter of heaven has appeared opposite, clothing herself in light, [coming] together from the east; she follows well the path of ṛta (the cosmic order) — like one who knows-forth (prajānatī), she does not confuse the directions.
stages.note: The Vedic seed of the term is verbal, not the abstract noun: the Dawn (Uṣas) is *prajānatī iva*, 'as one who knows the way', the present participle of pra-√jñā. What is known is the *path of ṛta* — the order one must not transgress. This is *prajñā* in germ: a discerning knowledge that orients action rightly, here cosmologised in the unswerving Dawn. The fixed feminine noun *prajñā* is not yet present in the Saṃhitā; the cognate *dhī* (illumined thought) carries the related load (see the *dhī* stage below).

stages.sanskrit: tat savitur vareṇyam bhargo devasya dhīmahi | dhiyo yo naḥ pracodayāt
stages.rendering: May we hold (dhīmahi) that excellent radiance of the god Savitṛ — he who may impel (pracodayāt) our thoughts (dhiyaḥ).
stages.note: The Vedic carrier of 'illumined thought' is *dhī*, not yet *prajñā*: the most-recited Vedic verse prays that the Sun-god *impel our dhiyaḥ*, our thoughts/insights. Aurobindo reads exactly this *dhī*-strand as the Veda's inner knowledge, identifying Saraswatī as 'that power of the Truth which we call inspiration … Truth comes to us as a light, a voice, compelling a change of thought' (Aurobindo, *The Secret of the Veda*). His is an interpretive lens, marked as such; what the text plainly gives is the Vedic location of discerning thought under *dhī*/*manīṣā*, the conceptual ground from which the later abstract *prajñā* draws.

stages.sanskrit: yad etad dhṛdayaṃ manaś caitat | sañjñānam ājñānaṃ vijñānaṃ prajñānaṃ medhā dṛṣṭir dhṛtir matir manīṣā jūtiḥ smṛtiḥ saṅkalpaḥ kratur asuḥ kāmo vaśa iti sarvāṇy evaitāni prajñānasya nāmadheyāni bhavanti
stages.rendering: This heart and this mind (manas) — perception, command, discernment, knowing-forth (prajñāna), insight, vision, steadiness, thought, conception, impulse, memory, intention, design, life-breath, desire, mastery: all these are but names of prajñāna.
stages.note: The Upaniṣad collects the entire cognitive vocabulary — *manas*, *medhā*, *mati*, *saṅkalpa*, *kratu*, *smṛti* — and subordinates every one of them as a 'name of *prajñāna*'. Here *prajñāna* has become the comprehensive abstract noun for cognition-as-such, the ground of which the particular faculties are modes. This is the nominalising step the Saṃhitā had not taken; it sets up the identification in the next verse.

stages.sanskrit: … sarvaṃ tat prajñānetram | prajñāne pratiṣṭhitam | prajñānetro lokaḥ | prajñā pratiṣṭhā | prajñānaṃ brahma
stages.rendering: … all this is led by prajñāna (prajñā-netra), is established in prajñāna; the world is led by prajñāna, prajñā is its foundation. Consciousness/discernment is Brahman (prajñānaṃ brahma).
stages.note: The *mahāvākya* of the Aitareya, and the high point of the term's career: discerning consciousness is not merely a faculty but is identified with the absolute itself. Śaṅkara, glossing it, distinguishes the *upādhi*-conditioned cognitive operations (*saṃjñāna* etc.) from the 'pure *prajñāna*-form of Brahman' (*śuddhaprajñānarūpasya brahmaṇaḥ*) of which they are limiting modes — the seed of the Advaita reading carried in the per-school material. The Saṃhitā's verbal *prajānan* has become a name of Brahman.

stages.sanskrit: suṣuptasthāna ekībhūtaḥ prajñānaghana evānandamayo hy ānandabhuk cetomukhaḥ prājñas tṛtīyaḥ pādaḥ
stages.rendering: The one whose locus is deep sleep, become unified, a sheer mass of cognition (prajñānaghana), made of bliss indeed, enjoying bliss, having consciousness for its door — he is the prājña (the deep-sleep knower), the third quarter.
stages.note: The specialised noun *prājña* names the self in dreamless sleep: cognition no longer dispersed into objects but 'become one', a *prajñāna-ghana*, a solid mass of awareness. (Our GRETIL source prints 'evāndamayo' for the standard 'evānandamayo'; we restore the universally attested reading and flag the source typo.) This is the deep-sleep sense the per-school Advaita entry draws on; the *turīya* of Māṇḍūkya 7 is then defined by negating even this — 'not a mass of cognition' (na prajñānaghanaṃ), 'neither cognitive nor non-cognitive' — so *prājña* marks the highest *state*, not the final reality.

stages.rendering: When there is clarity in the supra-reflective [absorption], there is serenity of the inmost self. There, the insight (prajñā) is truth-bearing (ṛtam-bharā).
stages.note: Yoga makes *prajñā* a graded cognitive attainment of *samādhi*: as the subtlest absorption clarifies, there arises *ṛtambharā prajñā*, insight 'bearing the truth' — said at YS 1.49 to differ from insight born of testimony and inference because it grasps the particular as it is. Note the Vedic echo carried in the word *ṛtam*: the Dawn 'followed the path of *ṛta*' (stage 1), and here insight 'bears *ṛta*'. The resonance is lexical, not a claimed textual borrowing.

stages.sanskrit: sthita-prajñasya kā bhāṣā samādhi-sthasya keśava | sthita-dhīḥ kiṃ prabhāṣeta kim āsīta vrajeta kim || … prajahāti yadā kāmān sarvān pārtha mano-gatān | ātmany evātmanā tuṣṭaḥ sthita-prajñas tadocyate
stages.rendering: 'What is the description of one of steady insight (sthita-prajña), settled in samādhi, Keśava? How would the one of steady thought (sthita-dhī) speak, how sit, how move?' … When one wholly abandons all the desires that come into the mind (manas), content in the self by the self alone — then is one called of steady insight.
stages.note: The *Gītā* coins *sthita-prajña*, 'one in whom *prajñā* stands fixed', and glosses it in the same breath with *sthita-dhī*, 'of steady *dhī*' — yoking the late abstract *prajñā* back to the old Vedic word *dhī* (stage 2). The accent shifts decisively from *what* is discerned to the *steadiness* of the discernment: wisdom defined as imperturbability when desire is released. Śaṅkara reads the *prajñā* of the steady one as the settled certainty 'I am the supreme Brahman' (*ahaṃ paraṃ brahma iti prajñā*), folding it into the Advaita identity-knowledge.

translator_note: 'Knowledge' is too flat: *prajñā* is discerning wisdom or insight, the faculty that sees, rather than the stock of what is known (closer to *jñāna* as content). The same term anchors very different goals — Advaita's identity-knowledge, Yoga's truth-bearing absorptive insight, Buddhist insight into emptiness and no-self — so the English should not silently import one school's object of wisdom into another's. Where the accent falls on stabilized wisdom (the *sthita-prajña*), 'steady wisdom' is preferable to any rendering that suggests new information.
